summary: A short-form ad hook should land as a visible contrast before the viewer has time to scroll.
principles: First second shows visible contrast (before/after, contradiction, sensory mismatch) that creates an immediate comprehension gap.
The visual gap raises the question — do not explain the product in the hook. Let curiosity pull the viewer forward.
Resolve the contrast into the product promise by the reveal beat — the hook's question must have an answer.
Hook must land without sound — 85%+ of TikTok/Reels/Shorts viewers start muted, so the contrast must be visually self-evident.
The contrast should relate to the viewer's real pain or aspiration, not abstract product superiority.
Frame the contrast so the product is visually absent in frame 1 and naturally present by frame 2-3 for maximum curiosity payoff.
Contrast speed matters: the before/after gap should be perceivable within 0.5-1.0 seconds on a mobile screen.
The hook contrast establishes the visual grammar for the entire ad — every subsequent beat should echo or resolve it.
apply_when: The platform is TikTok, Reels, Shorts, or another fast-scroll placement.
The product has a demonstrable before/after, upgrade, relief, or contrast claim.
The audience needs to understand the promise silently before narration is processed.
avoid_when: The contrast would exaggerate the product claim beyond approved evidence.
The only available contrast is unrelated clickbait.
The brand requires a slow prestige opening with no abrupt visual interruption.
failure_patterns: Clickbait contrast unrelated to the product — viewers feel deceived and bounce at the 2-second mark.
Too many contrasts in the hook — cognitive overload kills the single-question clarity that makes hooks work.
Before/after that exaggerates beyond what the product can deliver — compliance risk and credibility destruction.
Visual contrast that requires narration to understand — fails on muted autoplay where 85% of viewers start.

cross_domain_notes.note: Hook sets the emotional baseline; the wave pattern starts from it. A high-energy hook demands a different escalation curve than a quiet one.
Extreme close-up or macro shot maximizes visual contrast in tight mobile formats; wide shots lose the detail needed for contrast.
A sharp audio cut (silence to texture) at the contrast moment reinforces the visual surprise and makes it land harder.

execution_techniques: Shoot the 'before' state in cool, desaturated tones and the 'after' in warm, saturated light for maximum visual contrast.
Use a hard cut (no transition) between before and after — dissolves soften the contrast and reduce impact.
Frame the product at the exact same position in both before and after shots so the eye locks onto the difference, not the camera move.
Add a single impact sound effect at the cut point between before and after to reinforce the visual contrast auditorily.
